title: Regulator-portal authentication failure during submission window
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the content of the submission; focuses on the authentication path that precedes it.
evidence: Saved search 'regulator_portal_mfa_failure' (real-time) alerts when an automation account fails MFA on a regulator portal inside an open-incident window.

compliance.assurance_rationale: An aborted notification-flow due to MFA failure is a root cause of missed SLAs; this UC prevents silent failure.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.33(1) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.5: Regulator-portal authentication failure during submission window.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.39.5' running on IdP audit logs (okta:audit, azuread:audit), incident_register.csv with open_status flag., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Same for NIS2 initial notification obligation.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.23(1) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.5: Regulator-portal authentication failure during submission window.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.39.5' running on IdP audit logs (okta:audit, azuread:audit), incident_register.csv with open_status flag., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: An automation account used for regulator submissions fails MFA during an open-incident window; UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Same account authenticates successfully; no alert.

dataSources: IdP audit logs (okta:audit, azuread:audit), incident_register.csv with open_status flag.
spl:
index=idp sourcetype=okta:audit OR sourcetype=azuread:audit earliest=-15m result=failure
| search [ inputlookup regulator_service_accounts.csv | fields user ]
| join user [| inputlookup incident_register.csv | where status="open" | fields user=regulator_user incident_id]
| table _time user incident_id src_ip failure_reason
description: Joins IdP authentication failure events for a named set of regulator-portal service accounts against the currently open-incident register. An MFA failure in this window is a blocking event for notification SLA compliance.
value: Converts a class of silent failures (an expired portal API key, a stale MFA token) that has materialised in past incidents into an always-on monitoring pattern.
implementation: (1) Maintain a named macro regulator_service_accounts listing the IdP principals; (2) run every 5-10 min; (3) SOAR pages the DPO on hit. Indexes required: `index=idp`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=azuread:audit`, `sourcetype=okta:audit`; Lookups: `incident_register`, `incident_register.csv`.
visualization: Time chart of portal authentications by account, table of failures, single-value 'successful authentications in open windows'. Key fields: `failure_reason`, `incident_id`, `src_ip`, `user`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `idp` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `incident_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('incident_register.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.33(1)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.33(1) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.5: Regulator-portal authentication failure during submission window." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `idp` under `okta:audit`, `azuread:audit`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain a named macro regulator_service_accounts listing the IdP principals. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** run every 5-10 min. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** SOAR pages the DPO on hit. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Regulator-portal authentication failure during submission window," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`idp`, sourcetype=`okta:audit`) with a subsearch on `user` across the last 15 minutes. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `user` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.39.5: Regulator-portal authentication failure during submission wi]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -15m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.39.5,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=idp sourcetype=okta:audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `user`, `result`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=idp sourcetype=okta:audit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table user, result, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.39.5: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time Chart Of Portal Authentications By Account (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Failures (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Successful Authentications In Open Windows' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when incident clocks are tight so you can meet notification duties even after hours.
